chunk: UK Chancellor to Address MPs on Iran War Implications and Energy Security

The Iran war's implications and consequences are significant, with a major unanswered question being how long it will last. The UK government is preparing to address these issues with a focus on economic impact and energy security.

The Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, will address MPs with a three-part message: an update on the war's economic impact, a promise to deliver new nuclear power stations, and a new anti-profiteering framework to tackle companies exploiting price rises. The government will also focus on renewables and nuclear energy.

Ed Miliband, Energy Secretary: 'Anyone who tells you that new licences in the North Sea will make any difference to price is not telling you the truth. Because gas is bought and sold on the international market and the price is set there.'

The government's actions may include targeted support for families if energy bills rise, but a universal support package is unlikely. The focus on renewables and nuclear energy aims to reduce reliance on international markets and mitigate price fluctuations.
PM warns against 'false comfort' of thinking Iran war will end quickly

The Iran conflict has escalated with missile strikes and responses from Israel and the US. The UK has been involved in discussions and has allowed the US to use its military bases for strikes on Iran. The conflict has impacted oil prices and the UK government is considering measures to support households with energy costs.

Sir Keir Starmer warned his team against thinking the Iran conflict will end quickly. He emphasized the need for a 'swift de-escalation' but also preparedness for a prolonged conflict. The US and Iran have held talks, which have been described as 'very strong' by Donald Trump. The UK government is holding an emergency Cobra meeting to discuss the economic impact of the conflict.

Sir Keir Starmer: 'We mustn't fall into the false comfort that there will be a quick and early end.' 'The immediate priority has to be a swift resolution of the conflict and delivering a negotiated agreement which puts tough conditions on Iran.' 'This is not our war and we are not getting dragged into this war.'

The conflict has pushed up oil and gas prices. The UK government is considering measures to support households with energy costs, including potentially targeted support and regulation to prevent profiteering. The government has announced a new anti-profiteering framework to help regulators root out price gouging.
Government Narrows Down Potential Sites for New Towns in England

The UK government announced a scheme to deliver a wave of 'new towns' in England, with the goal of building 1.5 million new homes by the next general election. The plan was first announced at the Labour party conference in 2023, with 12 locations initially recommended for consideration.

The government has shortlisted 7 areas for further consultation: Tempsford, Leeds South Bank, Crews Hill and Chase Park, Manchester Victoria North, Thamesmead, Brabazon and West Innovation Arc, and a site in Milton Keynes. 6 sites, including proposals in Cheshire and Devon, have been deprioritised from the scheme. Labour ministers aim to start building work on 3 sites before the next general election.
No direct quotes are available, but Housing Secretary Steve Reed previously stated that funding would only be allocated 'when we know exactly what we want to build and where'.

The scheme's success will be crucial in meeting the government's target of building 1.5 million new homes in England. Industry figures have questioned the viability of this target. The selected sites will undergo further environmental assessment and could deliver between 10,000 and 40,000 homes each.
HS2 high speed railway trains could be made to run slower than initially planned to keep costs down
The HS2 high speed railway project, designed to connect London to Birmingham, has faced delays and cost overruns. The project was initially planned to be completed by 2033 at a cost of under £100bn.

The government has ordered HS2 to consider lower speeds on the line. The project's chief executive, Mark Wild, was expected to announce a delayed completion date and increased costs, but this has been postponed until after the May elections.
None

Reducing the speed of HS2 trains could help keep costs down and avoid further delays. The project has been criticized for its high costs and delays, with the Transport Secretary describing it as 'an appalling mess'.
Labour's Lisa Nandy criticizes party for blocking Andy Burnham's by-election bid

Andy Burnham was blocked by Labour's national executive committee from standing in the Gorton and Denton by-election in January. The party finished third in the by-election, behind the Green Party and Reform UK. Burnham is the mayor of Greater Manchester and would have had to step down if he had stood in the by-election.

Burnham attempted to become Labour's candidate in the Gorton and Denton by-election but was blocked by Sir Keir Starmer and his allies. Around 50 Labour MPs signed a letter objecting to the move. Nandy expressed support for Burnham and criticized the party's decision.

Lisa Nandy: 'He's a friend of mine. He's my mayor, and he was my neighbouring MP for seven years. I think he's a huge asset to the party. And I've said before, I'll say again, I'll support him in whatever he wants to do.'

The decision to block Burnham from standing has sparked criticism within the Labour Party, with Nandy being the most senior figure to question the move. The incident has fueled speculation about Burnham's potential leadership ambitions.
Reforms to prison sentences in England and Wales to reduce short jail terms
The Sentencing Act 2026 aims to ease overcrowding in jails and improve rehabilitation. The new rules are part of the government's plan to address the population crisis in prisons.

From Monday, courts in England and Wales must stop handing out short prison terms of up to a year, except in exceptional circumstances. Judges can now suspend jail sentences of up to three years. The reforms also include changes to bail rules and deferment of sentencing.

Riel Karmy-Jones KC: 'The pressure on prisons will be lessened, with more places available for those serving longer terms... This is an important measure, as it will help to prevent them from reoffending when finally released, ultimately providing the best protection for the public.'

The reforms are expected to reduce the number of people serving short prison sentences, which are often ineffective in preventing reoffending. Government statistics indicate that over 6,000 people are currently serving sentences of up to a year, and the changes aim to address the high rate of reconviction among this group.
Farage's Suffolk Event Cancelled Due to Protesters
The local elections on 7 May will include those for Suffolk County Council and Ipswich Borough Council. Nigel Farage's party, Reform, was launching its local elections campaign in Suffolk.

Farage's event was cancelled due to protesters with banners and chants. A party spokesman cited security advice for the cancellation. Farage appeared on stage with Michael Holt, who defected to Reform from the conservative group on Babergh council.

Protester Sarah Fitch: 'There is a lot of misunderstanding of Reform's policies.' Hussan Jamil: 'I feel Farage hates us and is trying to kick us out.' Dominic Cummings: 'Farage's statements about making Britain greater again have resonated with me.'

The local elections may serve as a 'wake-up call' for the government. Farage claimed that elections were only taking place in Suffolk this year because his party had taken legal action against the government.
Amess family to engage with review into Sir David Amess' death

Sir David Amess, Conservative MP for Southend West, was stabbed multiple times to death in October 2021 while holding a constituency surgery in Leigh-on-Sea, Essex. He had been referred to anti-radicalisation scheme Prevent seven years before his death, but his case was closed in 2016.
The Amess family had called for a full statutory public inquiry into Sir David's death, but were offered an 'overarching review' instead. The review will be led by Sir Robert Buckland.

Katie Amess: "We welcome the opportunity to meet with him and ensure that the questions our family has been asking for years are finally addressed. However, we must be clear: what we have always sought is a full statutory public inquiry." Radd Seiger: "A review does not carry the same powers to compel evidence, to test that evidence in public, or to deliver full transparency and accountability."

The Amess family will engage with the review in 'good faith', but their calls for a public inquiry remain. The review's findings may address some of the family's concerns, but its limited powers may hinder its ability to deliver full transparency and accountability.
Campaigners celebrate after new town plans dropped

The UK government had proposed expanding the Cheshire village of Adlington into a new town, sparking controversy and protests among local residents. The plans were put forward by developer Belport and were opposed by local council leaders and residents, who argued that the site was not suitable for large-scale development.

The government initially proposed Adlington as one of its new towns last year. Local residents organised protests and a petition with nearly 19,000 signatures against the plans was presented in Parliament by local MP Tim Roca. Cheshire East Council ultimately voted to oppose the plans and wrote to the government, leading to the government's decision to drop the plans.

Campaigner Aysha Hawcutt said she was 'delighted' and 'thrilled' by the government's decision, while Simon Gleave, chairman of Adlington Parish Council, said the 'hard work had definitely worked'. MP Tim Roca described the outcome as a 'hugely important victory' for the community.

The decision is seen as a victory for local campaigners, who argued that the development would be inappropriate for the area. While the government deemed the site a 'credible development opportunity', it will not be taken forward as a new town. Developer Belport said the site 'remains a viable proposition' for delivering homes.
Hillsborough campaigners blame Mahmood for law delay

The Hillsborough Law aims to stop cover-ups by creating a legal duty for public authorities to co-operate with and tell the truth to inquiries. Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer pledged to pass the legislation by 15 April 2025, but it will not go through before the current session of parliament ends in May.

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood declined to meet relatives of Hillsborough victims, citing ongoing discussions with other ministers. Campaigners claim she is being 'obstructive' over a proposed amendment about the security services. The government says it wants to take time to get the law right while ensuring national security is not compromised.

Charlotte Hennessy, whose father died in the 1989 disaster, said Mahmood was being 'obstructive' and unwilling to have a conversation. Labour MP Ian Byrne expressed concerns that Mahmood may be blocking the law's advancement. Sue Roberts, whose brother died at Hillsborough, urged the prime minister to 'pull rank' and ensure the law's passage.

The delay in passing the Hillsborough Law has been criticized by campaigners, who accuse the government of indecision. The law's passage may be pushed back to the autumn, with campaigners hoping it will be passed in the next parliamentary session. Barrister Pete Weatherby KC said the government had 'timed us out' and made it impossible to pass the law within the current session.
Arizona Sen. Kyrsten Sinema Switches to Independent, Shaking Up 2024 Senate Map
Sen. Kyrsten Sinema, a former Democrat, has become an independent, still caucusing with the Democratic party in the Senate. This move has complicated the 2024 Senate map for Democrats.

Sinema's decision to become an independent was driven by her low approval rating among Arizona Democrats and the threat of a primary challenge. Her switch has made the electoral math more complicated for Democrats in Arizona and nationally.
None

Sinema's move has made it more difficult for Democrats to win in Arizona, potentially splitting the Democratic vote and allowing a Republican to win. The Democratic party already has a difficult map heading into 2024, with 23 of 34 senators up for reelection caucusing with the Democrats, including 7 in states won by Republican Donald Trump.
The Unsung Hero of the Biden Administration: Louisa Terrell

Louisa Terrell, director of the White House Office of Legislative Affairs, played a crucial role in the Biden administration's legislative wins despite a closely-divided Congress. Terrell has over 20 years of experience in Washington, having worked as a staffer for then-Sen. Biden on the Judiciary Committee.

Terrell's team helped pass bipartisan legislation on infrastructure, gun safety, and same-sex marriage, as well as party-line bills on climate change and healthcare. She also worked on the confirmation of Ketanji Brown Jackson to the Supreme Court.

Terrell describes her role as a 'conductor' for moving Biden's agenda through Congress, saying 'You want to be talking to committees, caucuses. Who's talking to leadership? Who are the up-and-comers? What's the floor action? What's running quickly? What's slowing down?'

Terrell's work has been critical to the Biden administration's legislative successes, and she is known for her ability to build relationships with lawmakers on both sides of the aisle. Her experience and credibility on Capitol Hill have helped move the president's agenda forward.
GOP Congressman Criticizes Speaker Johnson's Leadership

California Republican Rep. Kevin Kiley expressed dissatisfaction with the leadership of Speaker Johnson, suggesting that he has 'failed to lead.' This criticism comes as the Republican conference prepares for the 2026 midterm elections and potentially a future beyond President Trump.

Rep. Kevin Kiley voiced his concerns about Speaker Johnson's leadership within the Republican conference. This development occurs against the backdrop of potential changes in district lines in California and the GOP's strategy for the 2026 midterms.
Rep. Kevin Kiley: 'Speaker Johnson has failed to lead.'
The criticism of Speaker Johnson's leadership may indicate a shift in the dynamics within the Republican conference as it prepares for future elections and potentially a new era post-President Trump.
The Axe Files Podcast Episodes

The Axe Files with David Axelrod is a podcast that features in-depth conversations with newsmakers and thought leaders. The podcast has reached a milestone with its 600th episode and has featured a wide range of guests, including politicians, journalists, and experts.

Recent episodes of The Axe Files include interviews with US Ambassador to Japan Rahm Emanuel, conservative commentator Scott Jennings, Democratic Rep. Marie Gluesenkamp Perez, historian Jon Meacham, and GOP campaign strategist Chris LaCivita. The podcast has also featured milestone episodes, including the 600th episode, which was marked with a conversation with CNN's Anderson Cooper.

Notable quotes from recent episodes include: 'The need for conservative voices in mainstream media' (Scott Jennings), 'the importance of respect in winning—and keeping—voters' (Rep. Marie Gluesenkamp Perez), and 'the moral decisions we face as a country' (Jon Meacham).

The Axe Files podcast has provided a platform for in-depth conversations with influential figures, offering insights into politics, current events, and social issues. The podcast's milestone 600th episode highlights its enduring popularity and impact.
Margins of Error: Various Episodes

The article appears to be a collection of episode summaries from a podcast called 'Margins of Error' hosted by Harry Enten. The podcast explores various topics related to everyday life, science, and human behavior.

The podcast covers topics such as optimizing travel, underdogs, weather forecasting, romance, metrics, diet, happiness, glasses, bathing, and accents. Each episode features Harry Enten discussing these topics with various experts.
No direct quotes are provided in the article.
The podcast aims to provide insights and analysis on various aspects of life, encouraging listeners to think critically about the world around them.
Donald Trump's complicated set of court cases
Former President Donald Trump is facing a multitude of civil and criminal cases. He denies any wrongdoing and has pleaded not guilty in all of the criminal cases, alleging a 'witch hunt' against him.

Trump's $250 million civil fraud trial in New York; E. Jean Carroll defamation lawsuit; federal criminal court cases in DC and Florida; New York criminal court case on hush money payments; Georgia criminal court case on election interference; and various lawsuits against Trump
None

The trials and lawsuits could potentially cost Trump some of his prized possessions, including Trump Tower, and impact his presidential campaign. The cases also have implications for Trump's business and personal reputation.
Republicans and Democrats unite to protect same-sex marriage
The Respect for Marriage Act aims to codify same-sex marriages amid concerns that the Supreme Court may target them
The Senate passed the bill with 12 Republican senators voting with Democrats; it now heads to the House for approval
Sen. Shelley Moore Capito: 'I will be supporting the substitute amendment because it will ensure our religious freedoms are upheld and protected, one of the bedrocks of our democracy.'
The bill's passage marks a significant shift in Congress, with Republicans and Democrats working together to protect same-sex marriage, a reversal from the 1990s when they united to restrict it
The fate of President Joe Biden’s federal student loan forgiveness program lies with the Supreme Court.

The program promises to deliver up to $20,000 of debt relief for millions of borrowers. The program was launched on October 17, 2022, and 26 million people had applied by the time a federal district court judge struck down the program on November 10, 2022.

The Supreme Court heard arguments on February 28 in two cases concerning the forgiveness program, and a decision is expected by late June or early July. The program was stopped by a federal district court judge on November 10, 2022, and the 8th US Circuit Court of Appeals reversed a lower court decision and blocked the program.

Lawyers for the government say that Congress gave the secretary of education 'expansive authority to alleviate the hardship that federal student loan recipients may suffer as a result of national emergencies,' like the Covid-19 pandemic.

If the program moves forward, an estimated 8 million borrowers may receive debt relief automatically. Borrowers who have debt remaining after either $10,000 or $20,000 is wiped away could see their monthly payment amounts recalculated if they are enrolled in a standard repayment plan.
Sudan healthcare facility strike kills 64, wounds 89: WHO

The conflict in Sudan is between the army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF). The RSF controls the western Darfur region, while the army controls the east, centre, and north of Sudan. The war has resulted in tens of thousands of deaths, over 11 million displaced people, and 33 million in need of humanitarian aid.

A drone strike on the El-Daein teaching hospital in East Darfur killed 64 people and wounded 89. The strike is attributed to the Sudanese army. This incident is part of a series of attacks on healthcare facilities in Sudan, with over 1,800 people killed and 173 health workers since the start of the war.

UN human rights chief Volker Türk said: 'Parties to the conflict in Sudan continue to use increasingly powerful drones to deploy explosive weapons with wide-area impacts in populated areas.' He was 'appalled' after over 200 civilians were reported killed by drone attacks within an eight-day period.

The war in Sudan has caused a massive humanitarian crisis, with the world's largest displacement and hunger crises. Over 33 million people are in need of humanitarian aid, and 12 recent attacks on healthcare have caused 178 deaths and 237 injuries.
Madagascar's military president to require new ministers to pass lie detector tests

Michael Randrianirina took power in a coup in October after youth-led protests. He dismissed the prime minister and cabinet earlier this month. Madagascar is one of the world's poorest countries, with high levels of corruption.
Randrianirina dismissed the prime minister and cabinet on March 9. He announced a new prime minister, Mamitiana Rajaonarison, on Sunday. Randrianirina pledged to hold elections by late 2027.

Randrianirina: 'We have decided to use a polygraph... to carry out background integrity checks.' 'We're not looking for someone who is 100% clean, but over 60%.' Gen Z activist: 'It's not even scientifically proven to work... it's just a joke and embarrassing.'
The use of lie detector tests aims to root out corrupt candidates. Madagascar's corruption perceptions index ranking is 148 out of 180 countries. The country's GDP per capita is $545.
UK aid cuts: Poorest countries to lose out on funding for schools and clinics

The UK's foreign secretary has set out budget cuts that will reduce bilateral aid to African countries by almost £900m by 2028-29. The 40% reduction in UK aid spending will mean all aid spending being cut to all G20 countries except Turkey.

The UK's aid spending will be cut by £6bn, with 70% of support allocated to fragile and conflict-affected states by 2029. Countries such as Afghanistan, Somalia, and Yemen will face cuts, while Mozambique and Pakistan will have almost all their development aid cut.

Romilly Greenhill, CEO of Bond, said: 'Africa and the Middle East, both home to some of the world’s least-developed countries, will be forced to pay the highest price because of the reduced budget.' Yvette Cooper said: 'This for us is not an ideological step – it is a difficult choice in the face of international threats.'

The cuts will leave children, people with disabilities, and older people more vulnerable across several countries. Fewer girls and children with disabilities will be able to go to school in South Sudan, and cuts to programmes in Somalia will heavily affect access to health services for women and children.
Tanzanian Woman's Death Sentence Quashed After 10 Years in Prison

Lemi Limbu, a woman with severe intellectual disabilities in Tanzania, was convicted of murdering her daughter in 2015 and sentenced to death. She has spent over a decade in prison awaiting execution.

Limbu's conviction was quashed on March 4, and she will face a retrial. Her original conviction was nullified in 2019 due to procedural errors. In 2022, she was retried and sentenced to death again. A second appeal was filed and heard in February.

"She was not supposed to be in prison in the first place" - Anna Henga, executive director of Legal and Human Rights Centre. "Limbu has endured unimaginable suffering as a survivor of sexual violence living with intellectual disability. After spending more than a decade on death row, she should be released" - Prof Sandra Babcock.

Limbu's case has drawn international attention, with UN human rights experts and 24 human rights groups condemning her sentence. Her case highlights issues with the justice system in Tanzania, where the death penalty is mandatory for murder, and over 500 people are on death row.
Jihadist violence rises sharply in Nigeria and DRC, while global terrorism deaths drop to a decade low

The Global Terrorism Index, produced by the Institute for Economics & Peace (IEP), recorded a global decline in deaths from terrorism of 28% to 5,582 in 2025. However, Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo saw significant increases in terrorism-related deaths.

Nigeria recorded 750 terrorism deaths in 2025, a 46% increase from 2024. The DRC saw a 28% rise in terrorism-related deaths to 467. Notable incidents include a massacre of 162 people in Kwara state, Nigeria, and a coordinated assault on a military base in Borno state, Nigeria, which killed at least 80 fighters.

Steve Killelea, IEP's founder: 'Viewed in totality, these trends point to one sobering conclusion: a fracturing world order risks erasing the hard-fought gains made against terrorism over the past decade.' Ladd Serwat, ACLED's senior analyst for Africa: 'Ten [of the ISWAP incidents] involved drone attacks and the remainder were intelligence‑gathering or surveillance missions used to prepare ground offensives against military targets.'

The rise in terrorism-related deaths in Nigeria and the DRC contrasts with a global decline in deaths from terrorism. The Sahel region remains the centre of global terrorism, with over half of all deaths from terrorism worldwide occurring there. The report highlights a growing concentration of attacks in border regions, including the Central Sahel tri-border area and the Lake Chad Basin.
Air Canada Jazz flight attendant survives 100m ejection after collision with fire truck
The CRJ-900 jet, operated by Jazz Aviation, collided with a fire truck at New York's LaGuardia airport on Sunday, killing the pilot and co-pilot.

The flight attendant, Solange Tremblay, was ejected over 100m from the plane while still strapped to her seat. Nine people were sent to the hospital with injuries. The incident occurred during landing when the plane collided with a fire truck.

It's a complete miracle... her seat was ejected more than 100 metres from the plane. They found her and she was still strapped into her seat - Sarah Lépine, daughter of flight attendant Solange Tremblay. The loss of our two fellow crew members onboard Flight 8646 is a profound tragedy - Jason Ambrosi, president of the Air Line Pilots Association.

The incident resulted in the deaths of two pilots, Antoine Forest and Mackenzie Gunther, and injured nine people. The flight attendant, Solange Tremblay, suffered multiple bone fractures and underwent surgery on a broken leg.
Police in Rio de Janeiro arrest eight people for brutally beating a capybara

The capybara, resembling a giant guinea pig, is the world's largest rodent and often seen in Brazilian cities, particularly near streams and lagoons. It has gained a devoted online following, with a popular meme depicting it as a communist revolutionary.

A group of attackers beat a 65kg male capybara with sticks and iron bars in Rio's Ilha do Governador neighbourhood. The incident was filmed by security cameras and eight people, including two minors, were arrested. The capybara was taken to a wildlife care center for treatment.
Police Commissioner Felipe Santoro: 'This is a brutal crime that shocks society.' Veterinarian Jeferson Pires: 'I have never before received a capybara subjected to such extreme aggression.'

The capybara suffered from head trauma, internal bleeding, and multiple injuries. The incident has caused outrage in Brazil, following a similar wave of outrage in early January after a stray dog was beaten to death by teenagers.
Colombian military transport plane crashes, killing at least 66 people

A Colombian military transport plane with 121 people on board, mostly soldiers, crashed in the country's south. The Lockheed Martin Hercules C-130 plane was transporting troops from the armed forces. The plane was taking off from Puerto Leguízamo, deep in Colombia's southern Amazon region, on the border with Peru.

The plane crashed shortly after takeoff, killing at least 66 people. At least 77 people were rescued from the crash site with injuries. The air force and defence ministry are investigating the final number of fatalities.

Colombian President Gustavo Petro described the crash as a 'horrific accident that should never have happened.' Defence Minister Pedro Sánchez said 'the aircraft was in airworthy condition and the crew was duly qualified.' Carlos Fernando Silva, commander of Colombia's air force, said 'details of the crash were not yet known, except that the plane had a problem and went down about two kilometres from the airport.'

The Colombian government has sent rescue teams to the site and two planes with 74 beds to fly the injured back to hospitals. The incident has sparked an investigation into its causes, with officials ruling out an attack by armed groups. The crash has also prompted messages of condolence from presidential candidates and a commitment from Lockheed Martin to help with the investigation.
Air Canada Express regional jet collides with fire truck at LaGuardia airport, killing two pilots

The incident occurred on Sunday at 11:45pm ET, involving an Air Canada Express CRJ-900 plane carrying 72 passengers and four crew members from Montreal. The plane was operated by Jazz Aviation and was landing at New York's LaGuardia airport.

The plane collided with a fire truck responding to a separate incident on the runway. Nine people were hospitalized, and the airport was closed for several hours. The flight data recorder and cockpit voice recorder were recovered.

National Transportation Safety Board chair Jennifer Homendy: 'It's going to be days' to reopen the runway. Donald Trump: 'They made a mistake; it's a dangerous business.' Mark Carney: 'deeply saddening'. Sean Duffy: expressed condolences to families impacted. Jason Ambrosi: 'The loss of our two fellow crewmembers is a profound tragedy'

LaGuardia airport reopened on Monday at 2pm, but the runway where the crash occurred will remain closed for 'some time'. Passengers were advised to expect delays and cancellations. A ground stop was also put in place at Newark Liberty international airport due to reports of smoke.
Canadian Woman and Autistic Daughter Detained by ICE for Nearly a Week

Tania Warner, a Canadian woman, and her 7-year-old daughter Ayla Luca, who has autism, were detained by US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for nearly a week. The family, living in Texas, was stopped at a border patrol checkpoint on March 14.

The family was stopped at a border patrol checkpoint in Sarita, Texas. Tania and Ayla were detained and transferred to the Dilley immigration processing center in south Texas. They were originally held at the Rio Grande Valley Central processing center in McAllen, Texas.

Tania's husband, Edward Warner, said: 'She says she's traumatized … They're not good.' Vicente Gonzalez, a Democratic congressman, stated: 'Tania has a work permit and is part of the fabric of our Kingsville community; she nor her daughter Ayla, a 7-year-old with autism, should be in detention.' Heather Neufeld, an immigration lawyer, said: 'Getting people out has been incredibly difficult right now … in a lot of cases, the only way to get someone potentially out … is in federal courts – so nothing quick.'

The detention has caused trauma to Tania and Ayla. Ayla developed a rash during her time in detention. The family is scrambling to raise money for legal help. The Canadian government is aware of the case and is advocating for Tania's rights.
Applications Open for 2026 George Enescu International Competition

The George Enescu International Competition, first held in 1958, is a prestigious event for emerging classical musicians. It is part of the World Federation of International Music Competitions and celebrates its 20th anniversary in 2026.

The competition will take place in Bucharest from August 23 to September 19, 2026. It features four sections: violin, cello, piano, and composition. Participants will compete across multiple rounds at key venues, including the National University of Music and the Romanian Athenaeum.
No quotes available

Winners gain access to a €150,000 prize fund, performances with major orchestras, mentorship, and the chance to perform at future editions of the George Enescu International Festival. The competition is a launchpad for global recognition, offering career opportunities and exposure to top industry figures.
The European Parliament adopted its first-ever report on the Housing Crisis in the European Union.

The European Parliament has adopted a report on the housing crisis in the EU, calling for joint action to tackle the issue. The crisis worsened after the 2008 global financial crisis and the 2020 pandemic, with house prices rising by 60.5% from 2015 and rents increasing by 28%.

The report recommends reducing red tape, scaling up construction and innovation sectors, and mobilizing greater investment and fiscal support. The EU's Affordable Housing Plan aims to boost house supply, mobilize investments, and help citizens with immediate support mechanisms.

"In many countries' salaries have not kept up with inflation. Then the real estate market pushed prices up to 60 percent in 10 years. This is not citizens' fault. It’s a lack of public policy, attention, and monitoring", MEP Irene Tinagli told Euronews. Elizabeth Kuiper warns that a multi-level cooperation with local authorities is fundamental.

The housing crisis affects not only the most vulnerable but also working individuals and families who cannot secure stable housing. The EU's actions can only complement national policies, but it can indirectly influence housing through funding and soft-law tools. The impact of the EU's efforts remains to be seen, but cooperation with member states is central to facing the ongoing crisis.
Anthropic to argue against US government in court over AI restrictions

The standoff between Anthropic and the US government began in February when President Donald Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced the termination of ties with Anthropic due to the company's refusal to allow unrestricted military use of its Claude AI model.

Anthropic filed two lawsuits against the government on March 9, one for reconsideration of the Pentagon's designation and another arguing that the blacklisting raises concerns over the First Amendment. The company will argue its case for a preliminary injunction against the Department of War and the White House in federal court on Tuesday.

Anthropic's co-founder and CEO Dario Amodei said that the company has 'no choice but to challenge' the designation in court. The Department of War argued that it was concerned Anthropic might 'attempt to disable its technology or preemptively alter the behaviour of its model' before or during 'warfighting operations'.

The case has broader implications for the AI industry, with AI scientists and researchers from companies like OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft filing briefs in support of Anthropic. The Pentagon has shifted its attention to work with other AI companies, including OpenAI, xAI, and Google.
Traditional beach breaks to be overtaken by active holidays and 'darecations'

The travel industry is shifting towards active holidays and 'darecations' as travellers seek adrenaline rushes and outdoor adventures over traditional beach breaks. Pinterest has highlighted 'darecations' as one of its biggest travel trends for 2026, with searches for adventure tourism surging 75% on the platform.

The European adventure travel market is estimated to be worth $464 billion. Searches for adventure tourism surged 75% on Pinterest. Trekking and mountaineering saw a 69% rise year-on-year, while marathon running holidays saw a 56% annual rise. Sailing and white-water rafting also saw significant increases in popularity.

“Me and my partner often choose to go on non-stereotypical holidays… We find that holidays like this do our mental health the world of good, over sitting about for a week.” - Sophie Barker. “It’s important to understand the risks associated with each activity, ensuring you have the right equipment and choosing specialist travel insurance to match your specific sport.” - Chris Trotman. “European travellers are highly experienced and increasingly motivated by meaningful travel… They want to connect with local culture, spend time in nature and feel that their travel choices benefit the places they visit.” - Russell Walters

The shift towards active holidays is expected to have a significant impact on the travel industry, with travellers seeking more cultural immersion and meaningful experiences. The industry is also seeing a rise in demand for specialist travel insurance for niche adventure sports.
Euronews' Flagship Shows and Initiatives
Euronews offers various shows covering news, politics, technology, food, water, and climate
Euronews has multiple flagship shows and series, including Europe Today, The Ring, Euronews Tech Talks, The Food Detectives, Water Matters, and Climate Now
These shows aim to provide insights into European issues, drive conversations, and explore the impact of new technologies and environmental challenges
One in five cases of tuberculosis in the European region goes undiagnosed

Tuberculosis is the leading cause of death from a single infectious agent worldwide. It is an infectious disease that spreads from person to person when an individual with pulmonary TB expels bacteria into the air by coughing.

Incidence across the WHO European Region has fallen by 39 percent since 2015, and the number of deaths by 49 percent. However, both figures fall short of the WHO'S End TB Strategy's 2025 milestones. In 2024, more than 160,000 newly diagnosed TB cases were reported in the region while the estimated number of actual cases was 204,000.

"By investing in rapid diagnosis, shorter all-oral treatment regimens and stronger follow-up, countries can reach more people earlier, improve outcomes and put us back on track toward our targets," - Hans Kluge, WHO Regional Director for Europe. "We must be prepared for this by strengthening treatment and prevention services, ensuring easy access to new medicines and fostering cross-border cooperation," - Ralf Otto-Knapp, German Central Committee against Tuberculosis.

The high number of undiagnosed cases and growing antibiotic resistance pose significant challenges to TB elimination in the European region. Thousands of new infections and deaths could be prevented with improved detection and treatment.
Are state pensions sufficient to meet the cost of living in Europe?

Research by payroll and HR firm Moorepay analyzed state pensions in 39 European countries, including EU members, candidate countries, EFTA countries, and the UK, to determine if they are sufficient to cover living costs.
The study found that in 20 out of 39 European countries, state pensions do not cover living costs, excluding rent. Pensions as a share of living costs range from 22% in Georgia to 225% in Luxembourg.

Noel Whiteside, visiting professor at the University of Oxford: 'some EU countries are simply poorer than others and require families to subsidise the pension income of elderly relatives and help out.' David Sinclair, chief executive of the International Longevity Centre UK: 'each nation's pension architecture is a significant driver of pension levels.'

The study highlights a regional gap, with Northern and Western Europe having pensions that often cover or exceed basic living costs, while Eastern Europe and the Balkans have pensions that usually cover only part of these costs. Two-thirds of the income of people aged 65 and over in Europe comes from public transfers.
Can AI save healthcare from collapse?
The integration of AI in healthcare is expected to transform the industry, but there are concerns about its seamless integration into current healthcare models and the safety of health and data.
Dr. Ricardo Baptista Leite, CEO of HealthAI, discusses the potential of AI in healthcare, the need for systemic change, and the importance of trust, regulation, and governance in AI adoption.

“AI presents to us an opportunity to ensure that everyone can have access to the health they need.” - Dr. Ricardo Baptista Leite; “If you retrofit AI into our current models of care, you have a risk of becoming very efficient at being inefficient.” - Dr. Ricardo Baptista Leite

AI has the potential to improve health outcomes, increase efficiency, and reduce costs in healthcare, but it requires significant investment, regulatory support, and trust from patients and professionals.
Only 14 per cent of cities worldwide breathe safe air
The World Air Quality Report analysed data from 9,446 cities in 143 countries. The WHO sets safe limits for PM2.5 or fine particulate matter due to health risks.
Wildfire smoke and extreme weather events drove poor air quality in 2025. Europe saw record-breaking wildfires, causing €43 billion in economic losses.
"Without monitoring, we cannot fully understand what's in the air we breathe. Expanding access to real-time data empowers communities to act." - IQAir Global CEO Frank Hammes

Only 13 countries met the WHO's safe guidelines. 130 countries did not meet safe guidelines. Air quality data gaps remain, with only a fraction of the global population having access to hyper-local, real-time air quality information.
EU and Australia seal free-trade agreement, slashing tariffs on most goods

The EU-Australia free-trade agreement aims to diversify trade ties and lock in strategic partners amid rising global tensions. The EU has prioritized the deal as it builds partnerships in the Indo-Pacific region.

The agreement will slash tariffs on most EU goods and farm exports, saving the EU €1 billion a year in duties. Tariffs will eventually fall to zero on products including cheese, wine, and processed foods. Australia agreed to quotas of 30,600 and 25,000 tonnes a year for beef and sheep respectively.

“The EU and Australia may be geographically far apart but we couldn't be closer in terms of how we see the world,” von der Leyen said. “With these dynamic new partnerships on security and defence, as well as trade, we are moving even closer together.”

The agreement is expected to increase EU exports by up to 33% over the next decade, with strong gains in key sectors such as dairy, motor vehicles, and chemicals. The deal also opens access to Australia's critical raw materials, including aluminium, lithium, and manganese.
Colombian Air Force Plane Crash Death Toll Nearly Doubles to 66

The incident was one of the deadliest accidents in recent history for Colombia's Air Force. The plane, a Lockheed Martin-built Hercules C-130, was taking off from Puerto Leguizamo on the border with Peru.

The plane suffered an impact near the end of the runway, clipping a tree and catching fire. Residents pulled out survivors, and military vehicles later arrived at the crash site. The plane had 121 people on board, comprised of 110 soldiers and 11 crew members.

President Gustavo Petro said, 'I will grant no further delays; it is the lives of our young people that are at stake.' Firefighter Eduardo San Juan Callejas reported the plane clipped a tree as it was plummeting and caught fire, detonating explosive devices on board.

The crash caused dozens of injuries and nearly 66 deaths. The incident has sparked calls for an investigation and criticism of bureaucratic obstacles delaying modernization of the military. Lockheed Martin has committed to helping Colombia investigate the incident.
North Korean leader Kim Jong Un sets out policy priorities, vows to strengthen nuclear forces and views South Korea as most hostile state
North Korea's policy shift towards South Korea, dropping decades of seeking peaceful reunification
Kim Jong Un's speech to parliament, outlining security and economic priorities, including a five-year development plan and increased defense spending

Kim Jong Un: 'expanding a "self-defensive nuclear deterrent" was essential to national security, regional stability and economic development'; 'North Korea had already proven that maintaining nuclear forces while pursuing development was the correct strategic choice'
Pyongyang's hardening stance towards Seoul, increased defense spending, and potential implications for regional stability and international relations
People walk along the torii path at Fushimi Inari Shrine in Kyoto on Monday
Fushimi Inari Shrine is a famous Shinto shrine located in Kyoto, Japan, known for its thousands of vermilion torii gates

Iran denies negotiations with US as Trump postpones threat to bomb power grid

The conflict between the US, Israel, and Iran has led to a significant escalation in tensions, with threats of attacks on Iran's power grid and infrastructure. Iran had responded to these threats by vowing to target US allies in the Middle East.

President Donald Trump announced that the US and Iran had held 'very good and productive' conversations, leading to a postponement of a plan to hit Iran's energy grid. Iranian officials, including parliament speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, denied that any negotiations had taken place. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu stated that Israel would continue to press on with attacks in Lebanon and Iran.

Trump: 'We have had very, very strong talks. We'll see where they lead. We have major points of agreement, I would say, almost all points of agreement.' Qalibaf: 'No negotiations have been held with the U.S., and fake news is used to manipulate the financial and oil markets and escape the quagmire in which the US and Israel are trapped.'

The announcement of potential negotiations led to a sudden reversal in market trends, with share prices rising and oil prices sharply lower. The Brent crude oil benchmark fell to $99.94 per barrel, down 10.92% on the day. The conflict has resulted in over 2,000 deaths since it began on February 28.
EU Citizens Express High Levels of Concern Over Active Conflicts Near European Union

The war in Ukraine has entered its fifth year and the war in the Middle East continues, causing concern among EU citizens. A recent Eurobarometer survey shows that over 7 in 10 EU respondents are highly worried about the active conflicts near the European Union.

British military bases in Akrotiri, Cyprus, were targeted with Iranian drone attacks in early March. Cypriot President Nikos Christodoulides called for a discussion with the UK on the future of British RAF bases in Cyprus. The European Council acknowledged Cyprus's intention to initiate a discussion with the UK and stands ready to provide assistance as needed.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte: 'If anyone thinks that the European Union, or Europe as a whole, can defend itself without the US, keep on dreaming. You can't. We can't.'

Two-thirds of Europeans want the EU to play a greater role in protecting them against global crises and security risks. 89% of respondents say that EU Member States should be more united to face current global challenges, and 86% want the EU to have a stronger voice at the international level.

Iran launches new wave of missiles against Israel amid US talk of 'very good' dialogue

US President Donald Trump said 'very good' talks were underway to end the war between Iran and Israel, despite Tehran denying any dialogue had taken place. The conflict has resulted in over 1,500 deaths in Iran, over 1,000 in Lebanon, 15 in Israel, and 13 US military members.

Iran launched a new wave of missiles against Israel early on Tuesday. Israeli jets carried out overnight strikes on multiple neighborhoods in Beirut's southern suburbs, targeting Hezbollah infrastructure. A semi-official Iranian news agency reported that two energy sites had been struck by airstrikes in Isfahan and Khorramshahr.

Iran's speaker of parliament, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, denied 'no negotiations' were underway with the US. Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said messages were received from 'some friendly countries indicating a US request for negotiations aimed at ending the war', but denied any such talks had taken place.

The conflict has displaced millions of people in Lebanon and Iran. Financial markets rallied after Trump's comments, with oil prices easing and stock prices jumping on Wall Street. The S&P 500 rallied 1.1%, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 1.4%, and the Nasdaq Composite climbed 1.4%.

Tel Aviv Hit by Iranian Missile Attack, Four Lightly Injured

The incident occurred amid heightened tensions between Israel and Iran, with the Israeli military intercepting over 90% of incoming fire from Iran. US President Donald Trump had threatened to attack Iranian power infrastructure if Tehran did not reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

A direct strike damaged a building in an upscale Tel Aviv neighborhood, causing four people to be treated for light injuries. The Israeli military issued multiple warnings of incoming missile fire from Iran and Lebanon. Iranian state-run media claimed Israeli-US strikes targeted gas facilities and a pipeline in central Iran.

Tel Aviv mayor Ron Huldai: 'The building had been hit by a direct strike.' Iranian state-run media: 'The gas administration building and the gas pressure regulation station on Kaveh Street in Isfahan were targeted'

The strike caused extensive damage to the building, scattered debris across the street, and resulted in four people being treated for smoke inhalation. The incident highlights the ongoing tensions and potential for escalation between Israel and Iran.
Trump administration pays $1 billion to TotalEnergies to walk away from US offshore wind leases

The Trump administration has been campaigning against offshore wind and other renewable energy projects. TotalEnergies had purchased leases for projects off the coasts of North Carolina and New York in 2022 for a total of $928 million.

TotalEnergies will receive $1 billion from the Trump administration to walk away from its US offshore wind leases. The company will invest the money in fossil fuel projects, including a liquefied natural gas plant in Texas and oil and gas activities.

Patrick Pouyanné, CEO of TotalEnergies: 'Considering that the development of offshore wind projects is not in the country’s interest, we have decided to renounce offshore wind development in the United States, in exchange for the reimbursement of the lease fees.' Interior Secretary Doug Burgum: 'We welcome TotalEnergies’ commitment to developing projects that produce dependable, affordable power to lower Americans’ monthly bills.'

Environmental groups have condemned the deal as a 'billion-dollar bribe' to kill clean energy projects. The agreement is seen as a setback for offshore wind development in the US, while East Coast states are investing in offshore wind to boost affordable electricity supply.
Valerie Perrine, Oscar-nominated actress, dies at 82
Valerie Perrine was an actress known for her roles in Lenny and Superman. She faced Parkinson's disease with courage and compassion.
Perrine started as a showgirl in Las Vegas, had a close connection to the Manson Family murders, and gained fame with roles in Slaughterhouse-Five, Lenny, and Superman.

Stacey Souther: "She faced Parkinson’s disease with incredible courage and compassion, never once complaining. She was a true inspiration who lived life to the fullest—and what a magnificent life it was."
Perrine's death leaves the world 'less beautiful.' Her legacy includes iconic film roles and a courageous battle with Parkinson's disease.
Bennett Slams EU Leadership Over Iran Stance
Former Israeli PM Naftali Bennett criticizes European leaders for calling for diplomatic engagement with Iran, while Israel and the US take military action against the country's nuclear program.

EU leaders call for de-escalation with Iran; Bennett says Israel and US are combating a 'nuclear menace' from Iran; Spanish PM Pedro Sánchez condemns US-Israeli war as violating international law; Bennett describes Sánchez's government as 'abhorrent'.

Bennett: 'We are fighting your war, we expect your backing.'; Bennett on Joe Kent: 'schmuck, irrelevant...he will go down in the dustbin of history'; Bennett: 'Our objective is to totally dismantle the nuclear programme and ensure they never achieve a nuclear weapon.'
The conflict may escalate tensions between Israel, the US, and European countries; the war's objectives and timeline remain unclear; Iran denies talks with the US are happening.
Hungary's Foreign Minister Admits to Regular Contacts with Russian Counterpart

Hungarian Foreign Minister Péter Szijjártó has been accused of regularly contacting his Russian counterpart, Sergei Lavrov, during private EU meetings on foreign affairs. The Hungarian government had initially dismissed these allegations as fake news.

The Washington Post reported Szijjártó's contacts with Lavrov during EU meetings in Brussels. The European Commission has called on Hungary to clarify the matter, describing the reports as 'concerning.' Szijjártó has confirmed the contacts, citing the need to discuss issues with partners outside the EU.

"Yes, these issues must be discussed with our partners outside the European Union. I talk not only to the Russian Foreign Minister, but also to our American, Turkish, Israeli, Serbian and others before and after European Union Council meetings. What I say may sound harsh, but diplomacy is about talking to the leaders of other countries." - Péter Szijjártó

The revelations come as tensions mount ahead of Hungary's parliamentary elections, with implications for Prime Minister Viktor Orbán's Fidesz party. The issue also highlights Hungary's continued ties with Russia, including significant imports of fossil fuels, despite EU pressure to reduce energy dependency on Moscow.
European Union and Australia clinch trade deal

The European Union and Australia have agreed on a free-trade deal, aiming to strengthen economic ties amid global trade volatility. This deal follows similar agreements with South America's Mercosur bloc and India.

The EU and Australia have reached a free-trade agreement, liberalizing goods flows while keeping quotas on sensitive EU farm products. The deal is expected to save the EU €1 billion a year in duties and increase exports by up to 33% over the next decade. Meanwhile, US President Donald Trump's ambassador to the EU, Andrew Puzder, expressed optimism about the EU implementing its trade deal with the US. Additionally, tensions between the US and Iran eased slightly as Trump claimed 'very good and productive conversations' had taken place, though Iran's parliament speaker denied any negotiations had occurred.

Andrew Puzder: 'All the signs seem to be good... it really would be economic malpractice not to pass this, it's a great deal for the European Union and for the United States.' Naftali Bennett: 'We have to see what the deal is. If it achieves those goals, that's wonderful.' Hungarian FM Péter Szijjártó: 'Diplomacy is about talking to the leaders of other countries.'

The EU-Australia trade deal is expected to boost EU exports and save €1 billion in duties annually. The agreement also opens access to Australia's critical raw materials. However, the deal faced challenges, particularly in agriculture, with EU farmers pushing back against liberalization. The outcome of the US-Iran talks and the EU's efforts to diversify trade ties will have significant implications for global trade and geopolitics.
China Conducts Vast Undersea Mapping and Monitoring Operation Across the Pacific, Indian, and Arctic Oceans

China is conducting a large-scale undersea mapping and monitoring operation across the Pacific, Indian, and Arctic oceans, which naval experts say would be crucial for waging submarine warfare against the United States and its allies.

The Dong Fang Hong 3, a research vessel operated by Ocean University of China, has been conducting extensive deep-sea mapping and sensor placement in the Pacific, Indian, and Arctic oceans. China's seabed-surveying effort is focused on militarily important waters around the Philippines, near Guam and Hawaii, and near U.S. military facilities on Wake atoll in the north Pacific.

The survey data 'would be potentially invaluable in preparation of the battlespace' for Chinese submarines, said Peter Scott, a former chief of Australia's submarine force. 'They're paranoid about being boxed in to the First Island Chain,' said Peter Leavy, formerly Australia's naval attache to the U.S.

China's efforts threaten to erode the U.S. Navy's asymmetric advantage in its knowledge of the ocean battlespace. The extensive surveying and Beijing's growing undersea capability are 'symptomatic of China's rise as a premier maritime power.'
Japan to release oil from state reserves amid Middle East tensions

Japan relies on the Middle East for over 90% of its crude oil imports, most of which travel through the Strait of Hormuz. The strait has been effectively closed by Iran in the wake of the start of the conflict with U.S. and Israeli forces in late February, triggering a surge in crude oil prices.

Japan will start releasing oil from state reserves on Thursday, with a total of about 8.5 million kiloliters of oil to be sold from 11 storage bases. The government also plans to tap joint oil reserves of oil-producing Middle Eastern nations stored in Japan by the end of this month. Japan started releasing oil from private-sector stockpiles last week.

The peace and stability of the Middle East are extremely important for Japan and the international community. We will continue to make all necessary diplomatic efforts in close coordination with related countries and try to minimize the impact on economic activities. - Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi

The release of oil from state reserves aims to cushion the impact of the tensions in the Middle East on the Japanese economy and help lower the national average retail gasoline price to around 170 yen per liter from an all-time high of 190.80 yen per liter last week.
Man rearrested and indicted for murder of wife thrown from 9th floor balcony in Tokyo
Jun Takahari, 49, was initially arrested in 2021 for the death of his wife, Asaka, 41, but was released due to lack of evidence. The couple had a history of arguing and had discussed divorce.

Takahari allegedly strangled his wife on Nov 29, 2020, and threw her from their 9th floor apartment balcony. Initial investigation suggested suicide, but autopsy revealed strangulation and no evidence of suicide.
"It's been a long journey, but I feel relieved now, or rather, I'm at ease. It feels like we've reached the starting line to get justice for my daughter." - Asaka's mother
Takahari faces murder charges after being re-arrested and indicted. Asaka's family expresses relief at the development, seeking justice for her death.
Revolut reports record financial results for 2025

Revolut is a UK-based digital banking platform that offers multi-currency accounts, instant money transfers, and a range of banking services. The company has established a strong presence in Europe and has gained full banking status in the UK.

Revolut's revenue rose 46% to £4.5 billion in 2025, driven by higher income from customer fees and lending products. The company's loans portfolio grew by 120% to £2.2bn. Profit before tax climbed 57% to £1.7bn. The company has 68.3 million retail customers and 767,000 business customers.
2025 was another landmark year. We have built a diversified, resilient business that is profitable at scale, providing the foundation for our next phase of growth - Nik Storonsky, co-founder and CEO

Revolut plans to invest £10bn over the next five years, including the creation of 1,000 jobs in the UK. The company expects to reach 100 million customers by mid-2027 and is considering an initial public offering (IPO) that could value the company at around $100bn.
